honoree.name: Black Kettle
honoree.summary: Black Kettle was a Cheyenne chief born around 1803 who played a significant role in 19th-century Native American history. He was a key signatory of the Medicine Lodge Treaty, advocating for peace between his people and encroaching American settlers. He was killed in the Battle of Washita River in 1868, a conflict that marked a tragic end to his efforts. His legacy is remembered as a leader who sought diplomacy amidst violent conflict on the American frontier.

honoree.name: Kit Carson
honoree.summary: Christopher Carson, known as Kit Carson, was born on December 24, 1809, in Madison County, Kentucky. He was a renowned American frontiersman, trapper, and guide who played a pivotal role in the westward expansion of the United States. Carson gained fame for his work with John C. Frémont on expeditions across the American West and for his military service during the Mexican-American War. He died on May 23, 1868, in Fort Lyon, Colorado, leaving a complex legacy as both a legendary figure and a controversial agent of Native American displacement.

honoree.name: Angie Debo
honoree.summary: Angie Debo was an American historian born on January 30, 1890, in Beattie, Kansas, and died on February 21, 1988, in Enid, Oklahoma. She specialized in the history of the American West, particularly the history of Native American tribes and Oklahoma. Her notable works include 'The Rise and Fall of the Choctaw Republic' and 'And Still the Waters Run', which exposed corruption in the allotment of Native lands. She is recognized for her pioneering research that challenged traditional historical narratives.

honoree.name: Trooper Nikky Green
honoree.summary: Trooper Nikky Green was shot and killed after he stopped to check on a vehicle parked on the side of the road, which had its trunk and hood open. Trooper Green informed his dispatcher at about 7:00 a.m. that he was out with a possible disabled vehicle on the north side of Devol, about five miles north of the Texas state line. During the stop, Trooper Green discovered a suspect had been cooking Methamphetamine.  When he attempted to arrest the suspect, a struggle ensued, and Trooper Green lost control of his weapon.  The suspect shot and killed Trooper Green before fleeing the scene. When the Highway Patrol was unable to make radio contact with Trooper Green, a Cotton County Sheriff's deputy went to investigate and found Trooper Green alongside a rural county road about a mile from U.S. 70. The suspect was apprehended two days after the murder and was eventually sentenced to death. Trooper Green had served with the Oklahoma Highway Patrol for 6 years and was assigned to Troop G. He is survived by his wife, three daughters, and a foster child.

Haynes was inducted into the Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame in 1998 for his contributions to the sport. He passed away on May 22, 2015, in Dallas, Texas.

honoree.name: Henry S. Johnston
honoree.summary: Henry S. Johnston was an American politician who served as the 5th Governor of Oklahoma from 1927 to 1929. He was born on March 13, 1867, in Franklin County, Arkansas, and died on January 17, 1965, in Drumright, Oklahoma. His governorship was notable for being the first in Oklahoma history to end with impeachment and removal from office. He was a lawyer and educator by profession before entering politics.
honoree.education: University of Arkansas School of Law

honoree.name: Lyndon Baines Johnston
honoree.summary: Lyndon B. Johnson was born on August 27, 1908, in Stonewall, Texas, and served as the 36th President of the United States from 1963 to 1969. He is known for his 'Great Society' domestic programs and the passage of landmark civil rights legislation. Johnson previously served as a U.S. Representative, a U.S. Senate Majority Leader, and Vice President under John F. Kennedy. He died on January 22, 1973, in San Antonio, Texas, leaving a complex legacy in American political history.

honoree.name: E. W. Marland
honoree.summary: Ernest Whitworth Marland was an American politician and oil tycoon born on May 21, 1874, in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania. He served as the 10th Governor of Oklahoma from 1935 to 1939 and as a U.S. Representative from 1915 to 1917. Marland founded the Marland Oil Company and was a prominent figure in the early development of Oklahoma's petroleum industry. He is historically significant for his progressive policies as governor and his philanthropy, including the creation of the Pioneer Woman statue.

honoree.name: Billy Fairl Morgan
honoree.summary: Narcotics Agent Billy Morgan, Corporal Ronnie Fox, and Detective David Sheehan were killed when their plane crashed into Jack Fork Mountain in southeastern Oklahoma. honoree.name: William Ross Stewart
honoree.summary: William Henry Murray was born on March 21, 1869, in Connecticut and became a prominent American politician and lawyer. He served as the ninth governor of Oklahoma from 1931 to 1935 and was a member of the Democratic Party. Murray was a key figure in the Oklahoma Constitutional Convention and served as a member of the U.S. House of Representatives for Texas. He is historically significant for his role in shaping early Oklahoma state politics and his controversial advocacy for segregationist policies.

honoree.name: Jim Thorpe

Thorpe is widely considered one of the most versatile athletes in history and was a member of the Sac and Fox Nation.
